cards.text: When overloading a binary operator as a member function, the {{c1::left}} operand becomes the implicit {{c2::*this}} object, and you only need {{c3::one}} explicit parameter for the right operand.

cards.front: Which operators MUST be overloaded as member functions?
cards.back:
• Assignment operator (=)
  • Subscript operator ([])
  • Function call operator (())
  • Member selection operator (->)
The compiler requires these to be members.

cards.question: What does this member function operator do?
cards.answer: Overloads operator+= as a member function. Adds rhs.m_cents to m_cents and returns a reference to *this for chaining.
cards.explanation: Compound assignment operators modify the left operand (*this) and return *this by reference to allow chaining (e.g., a += b += c).

cards.text: Compound assignment operators (+=, -=, *=, /=) should return {{c1::*this}} by {{c2::reference}} to allow chaining and match built-in behavior.

cards.front: Why can't member function operator overloads provide symmetric operations like friend functions?
cards.back: Member functions require the left operand to be an object of the class (*this). So myObj + 5 works, but 5 + myObj doesn't (can't call int::operator+). Friend functions allow both operands to be converted symmetrically.

cards.concept: When to use member vs friend function operator overloads
cards.explanation:
General guidelines:
  • Member functions: =, [], (), ->, and compound assignments (+=, -=, etc.)
  • Friend functions: Symmetric binary operators (+, -, *, /, ==, <, etc.) and I/O operators (<<, >>)
  • Unary operators: Can be either, but typically members
examples: Cents& operator+=(const Cents& c);  // Member: modifies *this
friend Cents operator+(const Cents& c1, const Cents& c2);  // Friend: symmetric

cards.text: As a member function, operator+ is declared as: Cents {{c1::operator+}}(const Cents& {{c2::rhs}}) with only {{c3::one}} parameter, since *this is the left operand.

cards.front: What should operator+= return, and why?
cards.back: It should return a reference to *this (ClassName&). This allows chaining (a += b += c) and matches the behavior of built-in types where (a += b) returns a reference to a.

cards.question: What's wrong with this operator+ implementation?
cards.answer: operator+ should not modify the left operand. It should create and return a new object. Use operator+= if you want to modify in place.
cards.explanation: Arithmetic operators should not have side effects. They should return a new value, while compound assignment operators modify in place.

cards.front: Can you implement operator+ in terms of operator+=?
cards.back: Yes. Common pattern: implement operator+= first (as member), then implement operator+ using it: Cents operator+(Cents lhs, const Cents& rhs) { return lhs += rhs; }. This reduces code duplication.

cards.text: The assignment operator (=) must be a {{c1::member function}}. If you don't provide one, the compiler generates a {{c2::default}} version that does memberwise assignment.
